news_summary: The JUPITER supercomputer achieved a groundbreaking milestone by successfully performing a 50-qubit quantum simulation, setting a new world record.
news_summary_fr: Le superordinateur JUPITER a franchi une étape historique en réalisant avec succès une simulation quantique de 50 qubits, établissant ainsi un nouveau record mondial.
news_summary_es: El superordenador JUPITER ha alcanzado un hito sin precedentes al llevar a cabo con éxito una simulación cuántica de 50 qubits, estableciendo así un nuevo récord mundial.

articles.title: JUPITER supercomputer breaks world record with 50-qubit quantum simulation
articles.summary: Scientists in Germany have pulled off a staggering computing feat by fully simulating a 50-qubit quantum computer for the first time ever using Europe’s new exascale supercomputer, JUPITER. The breakthrough shatters the previous 48-qubit record and highlights just how powerful next-generation supercomputers have become.
articles.content:
Researchers at the Jülich Supercomputing Centre and NVIDIA have achieved a major milestone in quantum computing by fully simulating a universal quantum computer with 50 qubits for the first time. The accomplishment was made possible using JUPITER, Europe's first exascale supercomputer, which was officially launched at Forschungszentrum Jülich last September.

The achievement surpasses the previous record of 48 qubits, which was also set by Jülich scientists in 2019 using Japan's K computer. Beyond setting a new benchmark, the breakthrough highlights the enormous capabilities of JUPITER and could accelerate the development of future quantum algorithms and technologies.

Why Quantum Simulations Matter

Simulations of quantum computers play a critical role in advancing quantum research. Scientists use them to test algorithms, validate experimental findings, and explore how future quantum systems may behave before real hardware becomes powerful enough to handle such tasks.

Some of the algorithms researchers are interested in include the Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE), which can help study molecules and materials, and the Quantum Approximate Optimisation Algorithm (QAOA), designed for solving optimization problems in areas such as logistics, finance, and artificial intelligence.

The Enormous Challenge of Simulating Quantum Systems

Recreating a quantum computer on a traditional supercomputer is extremely demanding because the complexity grows exponentially with every added qubit. Each new qubit doubles both the memory and computing power required for the simulation.

A standard laptop can manage simulations involving roughly 30 qubits. Simulating 50 qubits, however, requires around 2 petabytes of memory, which equals roughly two million gigabytes.

"Only the world's largest supercomputers currently offer that much," says Prof. Kristel Michielsen, Director at the Jülich Supercomputing Centre. "This use case illustrates how closely progress in high-performance computing and quantum research are intertwined today."

The simulation models the detailed quantum behavior of an actual processor. Every operation -- such as applying a quantum gate -- influences more than 2 quadrillion complex numerical values, a "2" with 15 zeros. Those values must remain synchronized across thousands of computing nodes to accurately reproduce the behavior of a real quantum processor.

NVIDIA GH200 Superchips Enabled the Record

The breakthrough relied heavily on NVIDIA GH200 Superchips used within the JUPITER system. These chips tightly connect central processing units (CPUs) and graphics processing units (GPUs), allowing data that exceeds GPU memory capacity to be temporarily stored in CPU memory while maintaining high performance.

To take advantage of this architecture, engineers at the NVIDIA Application Lab -- a initiative between the Jülich Supercomputing Centre (JSC) and NVIDIA -- upgraded Jülich's quantum simulation software, the Jülich Universal Quantum Computer Simulator (JUQCS). The updated version, called JUQCS-50, can efficiently perform quantum calculations even when some of the data is transferred to CPU memory.

Researchers also introduced a byte-encoding compression technique that cuts memory requirements by a factor of eight, along with a dynamic optimization system that continually improves data exchange between more than 16,000 GH200 Superchips.

"With JUQCS-50, we can emulate universal quantum computers with high fidelity and tackle questions that no existing quantum processor can yet solve," says Prof. Hans De Raedt of the Jülich Supercomputing Centre and lead author of the study published as a preprint.

Expanding Access to Quantum Research

JUQCS-50 will also be made available to outside research organizations and companies through JUNIQ -- the Jülich UNified Infrastructure for Quantum Computing. Researchers expect it to serve both as a scientific tool and as a benchmark for evaluating future supercomputers.

The project was developed as part of the JUPITER Research and Early Access Programme (JUREAP). "Through early collaboration, hardware and software could be co-designed during JUPITER's construction phase, in close cooperation between Jülich experts and NVIDIA -- an important step towards realising the full potential of this exascale system," explains Dr. Andreas Herten, a member of the Jülich JUPITER project team and co-author of the study.

JUPITER receives joint funding from multiple organizations. Half of the funding comes from the European High Performance Computing Joint Undertaking (EuroHPC JU). One quarter is provided by the Federal Ministry of Research, Technology and Space (BMFTR, formerly BMBF), while the remaining quarter comes from the Ministry of Culture and Science of the State of North Rhine-Westphalia (MKW NRW) through the Gauss Centre for Supercomputing (GCS).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in quantum computing simulation, achieving a world record with a 50-qubit simulation using Europe's exascale supercomputer JUPITER. This advancement has broad implications for accelerating quantum research and developing future quantum algorithms, which can impact multiple fields such as materials science, optimization, and AI. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a tangible, meaningful technological achievement with wide scientific and societal relevance.
articles.personality_title: Europe’s JUPITER supercomputer simulates 50-qubit quantum computer, setting new world record
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Quantum computers use qubits to process information in ways traditional computers cannot. Simulating these quantum systems on regular computers helps scientists test and improve quantum technology before real machines become powerful enough.

**What happened** – Researchers at the Jülich Supercomputing Centre in Germany, together with NVIDIA, used Europe’s new supercomputer called JUPITER to fully simulate a quantum computer with 50 qubits. This is the first time anyone has done a complete simulation at this size, surpassing the old record of 48 qubits set in 2019.

**Impact** – Simulating 50 qubits is extremely difficult because each extra qubit doubles the computing power needed. JUPITER handled this by using special NVIDIA chips and new software that compresses data and shares it efficiently between processors. This achievement shows how powerful modern supercomputers have become and will help scientists develop new quantum algorithms that could improve fields like chemistry, logistics, and artificial intelligence.

**What's next step** – The new simulation software, called JUQCS-50, will be shared with other researchers and companies through a platform named JUNIQ. This will allow more people to test quantum ideas and prepare for future quantum computers. The project also demonstrates the benefits of working closely on hardware and software together during supercomputer development.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Europe’s JUPITER supercomputer has set a new world record by simulating a 50-qubit quantum computer, paving the way for faster progress in quantum research and technology.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le superordinateur JUPITER d'Europe simule un ordinateur quantique à 50 qubits, établissant un nouveau record mondial
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les ordinateurs quantiques utilisent des qubits pour traiter l'information d'une manière différente des ordinateurs traditionnels. Simuler ces systèmes quantiques sur des ordinateurs classiques aide les scientifiques à tester et améliorer la technologie quantique avant que les machines réelles ne soient assez puissantes.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs du Centre de supercalcul Jülich en Allemagne, avec NVIDIA, ont utilisé le nouveau superordinateur européen JUPITER pour simuler entièrement un ordinateur quantique de 50 qubits. C’est la première fois qu’une simulation complète de cette taille est réalisée, dépassant l'ancien record de 48 qubits établi en 2019.

**Impact** – Simuler 50 qubits est très difficile car chaque qubit supplémentaire double la puissance de calcul nécessaire. JUPITER a réussi grâce à des puces spéciales NVIDIA et un nouveau logiciel qui compresse les données et les partage efficacement entre les processeurs. Cette réussite montre la puissance des supercalculateurs modernes et aidera à développer de nouveaux algorithmes quantiques pouvant améliorer la chimie, la logistique ou l’intelligence artificielle.

**Prochaine étape** – Le nouveau logiciel de simulation, appelé JUQCS-50, sera mis à disposition d’autres chercheurs et entreprises via une plateforme nommée JUNIQ. Cela permettra à plus de personnes de tester des idées quantiques et de préparer les futurs ordinateurs quantiques. Le projet montre aussi l’intérêt d’une collaboration étroite entre matériel et logiciel pendant la création du superordinateur.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le superordinateur JUPITER d'Europe a établi un nouveau record mondial en simulant un ordinateur quantique à 50 qubits, ouvrant la voie à un progrès plus rapide dans la recherche et la technologie quantiques.
articles.personality_title_es: El superordenador JUPITER de Europa simula una computadora cuántica de 50 qubits, estableciendo un nuevo récord mundial
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las computadoras cuánticas usan qubits para procesar información de forma diferente a las computadoras tradicionales. Simular estos sistemas cuánticos en computadoras normales ayuda a los científicos a probar y mejorar la tecnología cuántica antes de que las máquinas reales sean lo suficientemente potentes.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del Centro de Supercomputación Jülich en Alemania, junto con NVIDIA, usaron el nuevo superordenador europeo llamado JUPITER para simular completamente una computadora cuántica con 50 qubits. Esta es la primera vez que se logra una simulación completa de este tamaño, superando el récord anterior de 48 qubits establecido en 2019.

**Impacto** – Simular 50 qubits es muy difícil porque cada qubit adicional duplica la potencia de cálculo necesaria. JUPITER logró esto usando chips especiales de NVIDIA y un nuevo software que comprime datos y los distribuye eficientemente entre los procesadores. Este logro muestra la gran capacidad de los superordenadores modernos y ayudará a desarrollar nuevos algoritmos cuánticos que pueden mejorar áreas como la química, la logística y la inteligencia artificial.

**Próximo paso** – El nuevo software de simulación, llamado JUQCS-50, estará disponible para otros investigadores y empresas a través de una plataforma llamada JUNIQ. Esto permitirá que más personas prueben ideas cuánticas y se preparen para futuras computadoras cuánticas. El proyecto también demuestra los beneficios de trabajar juntos en hardware y software durante el desarrollo del superordenador.

**Frase clave** – El superordenador JUPITER de Europa ha establecido un nuevo récord mundial al simular una computadora cuántica de 50 qubits, abriendo el camino para un progreso más rápido en la investigación y tecnología cuánticas.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant, detailed painting of an immense, glowing quantum circuit shaped like a radiant 50-qubit lattice floating above an abstract, stylized supercomputer composed of interconnected golden and silver nodes and flowing data streams, all rendered in soft natural hues of blue, green, and warm amber, symbolizing the breakthrough fusion of quantum simulation and exascale computing power.
